dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for stroke.
Hi, I'm Anna, an EMT. What made you call 911 for the patient today?
She has been acting strangely and exhibited confused behavior for the past hour.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name?
Her eyes are open and she appears alert but visibly disoriented.
She responds with confusion, unaware of exact time or location.
Can you squeeze my hand for me when I ask, please?
Airway is clear, unobstructed, and maintainable.
She is breathing spontaneously and appears to be in no distress.
Her circulation is normal. Skin is pink, warm, and dry.
What is the last place you remember being?
Do you know what medications the patient is taking?
Have you noticed any signs of drug or alcohol use recently?
She exhibited hostility at times, screaming and running around the party area.
Are you currently experiencing any pain or discomfort?
I will quickly examine your skin for any rashes or bruises. Is that okay?
Pupils are 4 mm and non-reactive with no tracheal deviation.
BFAST exam performed but was inconclusive. No external abnormalities noted on the back.
I will check your movements and coordination for clarity and intent.
Patient shows acute erratic behavior with no focal weakness observed during limb testing.
The patient is normocephalic with no injuries or abnormalities observed in the head.
Her heart rate is currently 134 beats per minute.
She has a respiratory rate of 22 per minute.
Her blood pressure is 163 over 95 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation is at 96 percent.
Her initial blood sugar was recorded at 126 mg/dL.
I'm attaching a nasal cannula to monitor her end-tidal CO2.
We are administering a medication to help manage her behavior. It should help her calm down.
She's receiving 5 mg of Versed via intramuscular in the left deltoid muscle.
For our safety and yours, we're applying restraints using cravats on her upper extremities.
We are following protocol for behavioral management, as per standing orders.
Her heart rate has decreased to 109.
Her respiratory rate has increased to 27 breaths per minute.
Her blood pressure is 100 over 65 mmHg now.
EKG was not successfully recorded but 4-lead monitoring remains active.
Her glucose is currently unrecordable due to technical issues.
Latest EKG manually interpreted as sinus tachycardia.
Her pulse is 121, 18 breaths, and 116 over 74 blood pressure.
Oximetry remains stable at 97 percent.
The 4-lead ECG continues to show consistent sinus tach readings.
May I ask, which hospital would you prefer for your care?
[COMPANY_NAME] is fine. She seems calmer now.
We'll lift her to the stretcher using a two-person carry and secure her for transit.
We are en route to [COMPANY_NAME] for stroke and behavioral assessment.
The patient had abrupt disorientation and behavioral changes at the time of arrival.
Possible drug use was reported by family, although medical history is unclear.
Midazolam was administered intramuscularly, and physical restraints applied for safety.
A 4-lead ECG was performed and showed sinus tachycardia during monitoring.
She was stable and showed improved cooperation following restraint and medication.
There is no report of pain at this time.
Did she have anything to eat or drink before events occurred?
She just had orange juice, about 45 minutes before the incident.
Could you describe what occurred before the 911 call?
She suddenly started laughing and then ran around the house, then sat still on the chair.
She gradually calmed and sat down after the episode, then was restrained.
Can you tell me more about her past medical history?
She may have had diabetes, but we are unsure and could not confirm.
Her blood glucose was not readable on the second attempt.
Pulse ox is 92 percent at the most recent measurement.
GCS is 14-eye 4, verbal 4, motor 6.
She has no difficulty understanding or following verbal commands.
HEENT is normal with no discharge or observable hearing issues noted.
Breath sounds are not currently being assessed but appear equal and clear at this time.
Extremities show no angulation or deformity to our visual examination.
Abdomen is soft and without rigidity or tenderness on palpation.
Pelvic assessment did not show instability or movement issues.
Visual inspection of the back found no scars or physical trauma.
Skin is warm, dry, and shows no jaundice or rashes; I observed no paleness.
No bite signs or skin irritation observed at this time.
Patient shows no aphasia but has large gaps in awareness.
No jugular vein distention observed during initial and follow-up exams.
I will secure the ECG leads for continuous tracking of rhythm.
Midazolam (Versed) was administered intramuscularly at 5 mg dose.
Restraints are in place, per protocol for behavioral management.
She is now seated and positioned more comfortably.
ETCO2 and ECG are functioning, showing no changes to baseline.
Nasal cannula remains in place and is not causing irritation.
Restraint reported as necessary for safety and remains secure.
The patient is now calmer, restrained, more cooperative, and showing better compliance.
All vitals are stable, and behaviors have improved. She is ready to be moved.
We'll assist with the patient's movement to the stretcher with a two-person lift.
Patient is now secured, with straps in place for safe travel.
She's en route to [COMPANY_NAME] for full stroke and behavioral assessments.
EMS monitoring is ongoing and ready for rapid response if needed.
She remains breathing, calm, and without visible signs of distress.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the selected facility for acute CVA care and evaluation.
Oxygen saturation is now 92 percent.
BP is 116 over 74, and HR has increased slightly to 121.
EKG shows sinus tach, consistent with protocol and documented findings.
Events leading up to 911 confirmed, and discussion suggests possible poisoning.
Unable to confirm any known allergies due to limited patient contact.
Attempt to obtain past medical history was unsuccessful.
Last oral intake was juice about 45 minutes prior.
She is 62 years old, with initial disorientation on the living room chair.
Oxygen saturation started at 96% and declined slightly to 92% post-transport.
Medication was administered, receiving a noted improved response from the patient.
She was transported carefully; transfer was by manual carry to the stretcher.
ECG was completed, with readings interpreted as sinus tach at the scene.
All field care has been performed and is ready for hospital handoff.
EPCR is complete with intervention records and patient responses.
Patient is en route for stroke evaluation and further behavioral testing at the hospital.
She is secured and ready for emergency department arrival.
[COMPANY_NAME] will receive vitals, a 4-lead ECG, and clinical events.
We are less than 5 minutes from the hospital and entering [COMPANY_NAME] grounds.
The patient is currently with stable vitals, no pain reported, and is under restraint.
She is on the stretcher and not in distress. All records transmitted to EMD.
Handoff to the hospital team is complete and pending further reports.
We will stay with the patient until the ED team completes the room transfer.
The ambulance is returning to station and being restocked for next calls.
All field documentation is complete and submitted for record.
